title: Rate of child deaths attributed to child and maternal malnutrition
subtitle: The estimated number of deaths attributed to child and maternal malnutrition, per 100,000 children under the age of 5.
caption:
Rate of child deaths attributed to child and maternal malnutrition
The estimated number of deaths attributed to child and maternal malnutrition, per 100,000 children under the age of 5.

This choropleth map displays the estimated number of deaths attributed to child and maternal malnutrition per 100,000 children under the age of 5 for the year 2023. The visualization uses a continuous color scale ranging from light beige to dark maroon to represent mortality rates, with legend bands marking thresholds at 0, 10, 20, 50, 100, 200, 500, 1,000, and 2,000 deaths per 100,000 children. The highest mortality rates, falling in the darkest red bands above 500 and reaching toward 2,000, are concentrated across a broad belt of sub-Saharan Africa. Countries such as Somalia, Chad, the Central African Republic, Mali, and Burkina Faso appear in the deepest shades, indicating the most severe burden of malnutrition-related child deaths. Surrounding these nations, much of West and Central Africa, including Nigeria and the Democratic Republic of the Congo, sits in the 100 to 500 range. In Asia, Afghanistan and parts of Pakistan show elevated rates in the 100 to 200 band, while India and Indonesia appear in lighter orange shades corresponding to the 20 to 50 range. Conversely, the lowest rates, represented by the lightest beige colors in the 0 to 10 band, cover nearly all of North America, Europe, Australia, and the southern cone of South America. Greenland and several smaller island nations are marked with hatching to indicate no data. The geographic distribution shows a clear divide, with the highest values clustered in the Sahel and Horn of Africa regions, while the rest of the world predominantly occupies the lower end of the scale.
